1: davar YHWH el Zekharyahu ben Berekhyahu ben Iddoh hanavi: 'The word of the LORD came to Zechariah son of Berechiah son of Iddo the prophet.' The name Zekharyahu (H2148) means 'YHWH remembers' (zakhar = to remember, H2142). The remembrance-name is programmatic for the book: the book's entire message is that YHWH has not forgotten his people or his covenant promises, despite the desolation of Jerusalem and the temple. His grandfather Iddo (H5714) appears in the list of returning priests and Levites (Neh 12:4, 16), confirming the priestly family context. Zechariah is both prophet and priest — a combination important for his vision of the messianic priest-king (ch. 6).
2: qetsaf YHWH al avoteikhem qetzef gadol: 'The LORD was very angry with your fathers.' The oracle opens with a striking acknowledgment: YHWH's qetsaf gadol (great wrath/anger, H7110) toward the fathers explains the exile. This is the prophetic diagnosis that forms the ground for the call to repentance in v3: the exile was not a defeat of YHWH but an expression of his justified wrath. The fathers are contrasted with the present generation: the call is to not repeat what the fathers did. The opening frames the restoration oracles that follow within the context of honest acknowledgment of why the exile happened.
3: shuvu elai neum YHWH tzvaot va'ashuv aleikhem: 'Return to me, declares the LORD of hosts, and I will return to you.' The call-response formula of repentance — shuvu ... va'ashuv (return ... and I will return) — is the OT's most concentrated statement of covenant restoration theology. The same root shuv (H7725, to turn, return, repent) used for human return and divine return: both movements use the same word, implying that the divine 'return' (removal of judgment) is the corollary of human return (repentance). This formula appears also in Mal 3:7 (Return to me, and I will return to you). The NT's prodigal son narrative (Luke 15:20: while he was still a long way off, his father ran to him) dramatizes the same divine-response pattern.
4: al tihyu ka'avoteikhem asher qar'u aleihem hanevi'im harishonim laimor shuvu na midarkeikhemha ra'im umima'alileikhemha hara'im: 'Do not be like your fathers, to whom the former prophets cried out: Thus declares the LORD of hosts, Return from your evil ways and from your evil deeds.' The 'former prophets' (hanevi'im harishonim) are the pre-exilic classical prophets (Isaiah, Jeremiah, Amos, Hosea, Micah, etc.) whose message of repentance the fathers rejected — leading to the exile. Zechariah locates himself in the prophetic succession and calls the current generation to learn from their predecessors' failure. 'They did not hear, and they did not pay attention to me, declares the LORD' — the failure of prophetic preaching to move the previous generation is the cautionary background.
5: avoteikhem eyeh vehanevi'im hale'olam yichyu: 'Your fathers, where are they? And the prophets, do they live forever?' The rhetorical questions press the transience of both the generation that rejected YHWH's word and the prophets who brought it. The question 'where are they?' implies: they are gone, in the grave — but the word they rejected remains. This is the OT's version of 1 Pet 1:24-25: 'All flesh is like grass... but the word of the LORD remains forever.' The permanence of YHWH's word outlasts the generations that receive or reject it.
6: akh devarai vechuqqai asher tziviti et avadai hanevi'im halo higshu avoteikhem vayashuvu vayomru ka'asher zamam YHWH tzvaot la'asot lanu kiderakhenu uvema'alaleinu ken asah itanu: 'But my words and my statutes, which I commanded my servants the prophets, did they not overtake your fathers?' The word higshu (from H5381, to reach, overtake) — the divine word is like a pursuer that eventually catches up: YHWH's threatened judgments did overtake the fathers who ignored the prophets. The fathers' eventual acknowledgment ('as the LORD of hosts purposed to do to us... so he has dealt with us') is repentance after the fact — too late to avoid the exile but the foundation for the current generation's different response.
7: beesor vearba'ah livadash hasheni bishnat shtayim leDaryavesh: 'On the twenty-fourth day of the eleventh month, which is the month of Shebat, in the second year of Darius.' The date formula (February 519 BCE) is precise: Zechariah's visions came three months after Haggai's first oracle (Hag 1:1, August/September 520 BCE) and during the period of temple rebuilding. The historicity markers (regnal year of Darius, month name Shebat) ground the visions in concrete historical circumstances. The eight night visions that follow (vv8-21 and continuing through ch6) are received in a single night — a sustained visionary experience.
8: ra'iti balayla vehineh ish rochev al sus adom vehu omed bein hahadassim asher bameitzulah: 'I saw in the night, and behold, a man riding on a red horse! He was standing among the myrtle trees in the glen.' The first vision: the divine rider among the myrtle trees. Hadassim (myrtle trees, H1918) — the same root as Hadassah, Esther's Hebrew name. Myrtle was associated with blessing and restoration in prophetic literature (Isa 41:19; 55:13: instead of the brier the myrtle shall come up). The 'glen' or ravine (meitzulah, H4699 — depth, deep place) may represent the low valley of Israel's humiliation. The rider on the red horse is later identified as the Angel of the LORD (v11) — a divine figure.
9: vaomar mah eleh Adonai vayomer elai hamalakh haddover bi ani areka mah eleh: 'Then I said, What are these, O Lord? The angel who talked with me said to me, I will show you what these are.' The interpretive angel (hamalakh haddover bi, the angel who spoke with/in me) is a characteristic feature of Zechariah's visions — an angelic guide who interprets the symbolic content, similar to Daniel (Dan 7:16; 8:16) and later the apocalyptic tradition. The 'I will show you' structure introduces the explanatory dialogue that follows: vision, followed by dialogue, followed by interpretation. This is the standard structure for Zechariah's eight night visions.
10: vaya'an ha'ish ha'omed bein hahadassim vayomar eleh asher shalach YHWH lehithalek ba'aretz: 'And the man standing among the myrtle trees answered and said, These are they whom the LORD has sent to patrol the earth.' The patrol riders (shalach ... lehithalek = sent to walk about) are divine agents monitoring the state of the earth. The intelligence they return (v11: the earth is at rest) creates the problem: the nations are prospering while Jerusalem lies desolate. The divine patrol discovers that the settled condition of the nations is itself evidence of YHWH's continued anger — comfortable nations, suffering Jerusalem.
11: vaya'anu et malakh YHWH ha'omed bein hahadassim vayomru hithalaknu ba'aretz vehineh kol ha'aretz yoshevet ushaqatat: 'And they answered the angel of the LORD who was standing among the myrtle trees, We have patrolled the earth, and behold, all the earth remains at rest and quiet.' The patrol's report: kol ha'aretz yoshevet ushaqatat (all the earth sitting and quiet). The world's peace and stability — good in general — is here the ground for prophetic complaint: why should the nations be at peace while Jerusalem lies in ruins? The divine warrior's wrath against the nations (vv14-15) flows from this discrepancy.
12: vaya'an malakh YHWH vayomar YHWH tzvaot ad matai ata lo terachhem et Yerushalayim ve'et arei Yehudah asher za'amta zeh shivim shanah: 'Then the angel of the LORD said: O LORD of hosts, how long will you have no mercy on Jerusalem and the cities of Judah, against which you have been angry these seventy years?' The Angel of the LORD intercedes — he prays to YHWH. This is theologically significant: a divine figure intercedes with YHWH for YHWH's people. The seventy years (shivim shanah) echoes Jeremiah's prophecy (Jer 25:11-12; 29:10) — the period of Babylonian domination. The intercessory prayer 'how long?' (ad matai) is the lament prayer of the Psalms (Ps 13:1; 79:5; 89:46) placed on the lips of the heavenly intercessor.
13: vaya'an YHWH et hamalakh haddover bi devarim tovim devarim nichmuim: 'And the LORD answered gracious and comforting words to the angel who talked with me.' YHWH responds to the Angel's intercession with devarim tovim devarim nichmuim (good words, comforting/consoling words — H5150 from nacham = to comfort, console). These 'comforting words' are the substance of the following oracles: zeal for Jerusalem (v14), anger against the nations (v15), return to Jerusalem with mercies (v16), the measuring line (v16), overflow of prosperity (v17). The intercessory pattern: the Angel prays; YHWH responds; Zechariah announces. The NT parallel: Christ's heavenly intercession (Heb 7:25) produces divine comfort for his people.
14: vayomer elai hamalakh haddover bi qera leimor koh amar YHWH tzvaot qinneti liYerushalayim uleTziyon qinah gedolah: 'So the angel who talked with me said to me, Cry out: Thus says the LORD of hosts: I am exceedingly jealous for Jerusalem and for Zion.' Qinneti ... qinah gedolah (I am jealous with great jealousy) — the doubled root for emphasis. YHWH's jealousy (qinah, H7068 — zeal, jealousy, passionate attachment) for Jerusalem is the emotional ground of the restoration: he is not indifferent to his city's desolation but passionately committed to its restoration. The same attribute that made him angry at Israel's idolatry (Exod 20:5: I am a jealous God) now makes him angry at the nations' complacency about Jerusalem's suffering.
15: veqetzef gadol ani qotzef al hagoyim hashanannim: 'I am exceedingly angry with the nations that are at ease.' The nations' ease (sha'anannim, H7600 — complacent, at ease, arrogant) is their sin: they were YHWH's instruments of discipline against Israel, but they exceeded their mandate and became complacent about Israel's suffering. 'I was a little angry, and they furthered the disaster' — the nations interpreted Israel's punishment as license to inflict more. This is a significant theological point: divine instruments of judgment are themselves judged for their excess and complacency. The Babylonian empire's fall (612-539 BCE) was precisely this judgment against the instrument that exceeded its mandate.
16: lachen koh amar YHWH shavti liYerushalayim berachamim beiti yibbaneh bah neum YHWH tzvaot veqav yinnateh al Yerushalayim: 'Therefore, thus says the LORD: I have returned to Jerusalem with mercy; my house shall be built in it, declares the LORD of hosts, and the measuring line shall be stretched over Jerusalem.' YHWH's return (shavti, from shuv — the same return-verb of v3) to Jerusalem is the first specific restoration promise. The measuring line (qav, H6957 — the builder's/surveyor's line) stretched over Jerusalem means the city will be rebuilt — not destroyed as in Lam 2:8 (the LORD stretched out the measuring line, purpose: to destroy). The image is reversed: the line that once marked out for destruction now marks out for construction.
17: od qera leimor koh amar YHWH tzvaot od tifutzeynah arai mittov venichum YHWH od et Tziyon uvchar od biYerushalayim: 'Cry out again: Thus says the LORD of hosts: My cities shall again overflow with prosperity, and the LORD will again comfort Zion and again choose Jerusalem.' The triple od (again, still, yet) creates insistence: again prosperity, again comfort (nichum, from nacham = to console), again election (bachar = to choose). The theology of divine re-choice: the exile did not cancel the election of Jerusalem; YHWH 'again chooses' Jerusalem signals the permanence of covenant election that runs through temporary judgment (cf. Rom 11:29: the gifts and calling of God are irrevocable).
18: vaessa et einai vaere vehineh arba qaranot: 'And I lifted my eyes and saw, and behold, four horns!' The second vision: four horns and four craftsmen (vv18-21). Qaranot (horns, H7161) are symbols of power throughout the OT (Deut 33:17; Ps 75:4-5; Dan 7:7-8, 24; Rev 13:1). The angel identifies them as the nations/powers that scattered Judah, Israel, and Jerusalem (v19). Four is the number of compass directions — these are world-powers from all directions. The craftsmen (charashim, H2796) come to terrify them and cast down the nations that lifted their horns against the land of Judah.
19: vayomer elai eleh haqeranot asher zeru et Yehudah et Yisrael veYerushalayim: 'And he said to me, These are the horns that have scattered Judah, Israel, and Jerusalem.' The four scattering powers are not identified (Assyria, Babylon, Persia, Greece are the standard candidates) — the vision deliberately uses the number four to represent comprehensive, directional oppression. The verb zeru (from zarah, H2219 — to scatter, winnow) echoes the exile: Israel was scattered like grain on the threshing floor. The four craftsmen come to terrify these horns — the powers of scattering will themselves be overwhelmed.
20: vayar'eni YHWH arba charashim: 'Then the LORD showed me four craftsmen/smiths.' Charashim (H2796 — craftsmen, smiths, carvers) — those who work with metal or wood. Four craftsmen against four horns: divine workmanship against world-power. The image may draw on the ancient Near Eastern combat myth (divine craftsman defeats chaos) recast in terms of YHWH's historical actions through human agents. The craftsmen represent the successive world powers (Persia against Babylon, Greece against Persia, etc.) that YHWH uses to overthrow Israel's oppressors.
21: vayomer eleh ba'u leyarid et qaranot hagoyim hamerim qarnah el eretz Yehudah lezarotah: 'He said, These have come to terrify them, to cast down the horns of the nations who lifted up their horns against the land of Judah to scatter it.' The purpose of the craftsmen: to cast down (yadot, H3034 — to throw, cast down) the horns/powers of the scattering nations. The four-horns/four-craftsmen vision establishes that YHWH's sovereign control over history includes both the judgment that scatters Israel and the counter-judgment that overthrows those instruments of scattering. No world power — Assyrian, Babylonian, Persian, Greek — stands beyond YHWH's ultimate governance.
